title: Do Babies Matter? Gender and Family in the Ivory Tower By Mary Ann Mason, Nicholas H. Wolfinger, and Marc Goulden Rutgers University Press. 2013. 188 pages. $72.00 cloth, $25.95 paper
abstract: Mason, Wolfinger, and Goulden have provided data for over a decade to make the case that “the ideal worker model” still exists and prevents equity in institutions of higher education. The “ideal” individual works at least 40 hours per week year-round and bears no responsibility for housework and children (Williams 2000, 2). Do Babies Matter? Gender and Family in the Ivory Tower presents a comprehensive picture of how gender and family affect career trajectories, from graduate school through retirement. The authors should be lauded for this life course perspective and for integrating and analyzing several types of supporting data. They use sophisticated statistical techniques to analyze data from the National Science Foundation's Survey of Doctoral Recipients from 1966 to 2006, winners from NSF and National Institutes of Health awards, and responses to various surveys conducted under the auspices of the University of California. They also describe the kinds of policies that could bring about academic equity for women and men who care for children.
